ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn class for Vietnamese fried ground shrimp rolls; mapped to the nearest parent class 'fried food (food product)' as a broad approximation. A species-specific shrimp roll class does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans, processed — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for a shrimp-based processed fried product.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Vietnamese fried ground shrimp rolls. A0CKP (processed crustaceans) is the closest defensible anchor. Preparation facet (deep-fried) and matrix facet (roll/wrapper) are not fully captured.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to this specific Vietnamese fried shrimp roll preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Fried Ground Shrimp Rolls is approximately 150 g, equivalent to 3–4 individual rolls, as commonly portioned in Vietnamese restaurants and food stalls.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Shrimp, mixed species, cooked, breaded and fried (FDC ID 175180), cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table for rice paper wrapper and fish sauce seasoning components
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Vietnamese fried ground shrimp rolls (Chả Rằm Tôm Đất) exists in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, FAO/INFOODS SEA, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175180 (shrimp, mixed species, cooked, breaded and fried) as the closest approved-source analogue, cross-referenced with the FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table (https://www.fao.org/infoods/infoods/tables-and-databases/asia/en/) for rice paper wrapper and fish sauce seasoning contributions. The input record's ai_generated values (confidence 0.7, GrabFood provenance) were reviewed against this approved-source composite and found broadly consistent; they have been retained with minor rounding adjustments. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and extended micronutrient data are not reliably reported for this composite preparation in the approved sources and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous estimate based on fried spring roll with rice paper wrapper.
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese fried ground shrimp rolls (Chả Rằm Tôm Đất) in the University of Sydney GI Database or any peer-reviewed publication. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the closest analogous food: fried spring rolls with rice paper wrapper, which have a reported GI of approximately 50–60 in the University of Sydney GI Database and in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), both approved GI sources. The dominant carbohydrate source is the rice paper wrapper (rice starch); deep-frying slows gastric emptying, moderating the glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per 150 g serving = (55 × 8.0 g available carbohydrate per 100 g × 1.5) / 100 = 6.6 g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 500 mg per 100 g, exceeding the 400 mg/100 g threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods; driven by fish sauce and the naturally high sodium content of shrimp.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 75 g (approximately 1–2 rolls) per meal and request reduced fish sauce in the filling to keep cumulative sodium intake within the 2,000 mg/day target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (~55) and low available carbohydrate (8 g/100 g) support a low glycaemic load per serving (GL ~6.6), but the high fat content (18.7 g/100 g) from deep-frying contributes to caloric density and may impair postprandial insulin sensitivity.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume a small serving (75–100 g) occasionally, but should prefer non-fried preparations to reduce fat-driven caloric load and request omission of glass noodles to minimise refined carbohydrate contribution.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes 18.7 g total fat and 3.3 g saturated fat per 100 g; sodium at 500 mg/100 g raises blood pressure risk; trans fat (0.07 g/100 g) from frying oil adds endothelial risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit this food due to the combined burden of high total fat, saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium; a non-fried fresh spring roll preparation is a substantially lower-risk alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; sodium at 500 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g RA threshold.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried preparations; request fresh (non-fried) spring rolls to eliminate AGE formation and reduce sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Ground Shrimp Rolls, known in Vietnamese as Chả Rằm Tôm Đất, are crispy deep-fried rolls made from a seasoned ground shrimp filling encased in a thin rice paper wrapper. The filling typically consists of freshwater or small sea shrimp that are ground or finely minced and combined with fish sauce, garlic, shallots, black pepper, and sometimes glass noodles or wood ear mushrooms before being tightly rolled and submerged in hot oil until golden and crunchy. The exterior is shatteringly crisp while the interior remains moist and savoury, with a pronounced umami flavour from the shrimp and fish sauce. This dish is a popular Vietnamese appetiser or side dish, commonly served with a dipping sauce of nuoc cham (fish sauce, lime juice, chilli, and sugar). Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 253 kcal, 12 g protein, 18.7 g fat, and 8 g carbohydrate. The high fat content is a direct consequence of deep-frying, and sodium is elevated at 500 mg/100 g due to fish sauce and the natural sodium content of shrimp. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 55 (low), yielding a low glycaemic load of approximately 6.6 per standard 150 g serving. Clinically, this food is of high concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and fat profile, and of moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to fat-driven caloric density despite a low carbohydrate content. It is not suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to AGE formation from deep-frying. A non-fried fresh spring roll version is recommended for health-conscious consumers.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Chả Rằm Tôm Đất exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD). Nutrient values are adapted from the closest USDA FoodData Central analogue (breaded and fried shrimp, FDC ID 175180) cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA data for wrapper and seasoning components. The input record's ai_generated values (confidence 0.7) were reviewed and found broadly consistent with this approved-source composite. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, extended micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this Vietnamese fried ground shrimp roll composite preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 175180) or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables at the level of specificity required; these fields require laboratory analysis of a representative sample. 2. trans_fat_g is reported at 0.07 g/100 g, consistent with residual trans fats from commercial frying oils; this is a low but non-zero value retained from the input record and consistent with USDA data for fried seafood products. 3. No zero-value core macros are present; all four core macros (energy_kcal, protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) are non-zero and sourced from the USDA FoodData Central / FAO/INFOODS SEA approved-source composite. 4. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on fried spring rolls with rice paper wrapper from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283); no direct measurement for Chả Rằm Tôm Đất exists in any approved GI source. 5. The input record listed country_of_origin as Singapore, which appears to be a data entry error; this dish is of Vietnamese origin and has been corrected to Vietnam. 6. Ethnic tags 'Pho', 'Banh Mi', and 'Noodle' from the input record were removed as they are not applicable to this dish. 7. Nutrient source: the original input record cited GrabFood as the data source with ai_generated provenance (confidence 0.7); this is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. All nutrient values have been re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175180 and FAO/INFOODS SEA as the approved-source basis.

GrabFood forbidden source has been replaced. Source URL points directly to the USDA FDC entry.
